chunk_summary: Entitlement mapping under ICDS and POSHAN 2.0 ensures supplementary nutrition for children 6 months–6 years, with specific caloric and protein norms. Pediatricians must verify access, consumption, and address gaps like growth faltering or supply issues.
learning_objectives: Understand the nutritional entitlements under ICDS and POSHAN 2.0 for children and women.
Learn to assess and document gaps in supplementary nutrition delivery at anganwadis.
Identify and address growth faltering and malnutrition through programmatic actions.
Familiarize with NFSA nutritional standards and their implementation in pediatric care.
key_facts: Children 6–36 months receive take-home rations (THR), while 3–6 years receive a morning snack and hot cooked meal.
NFSA nutritional norms: 500 kcal and 12–15 g protein/day for children 6 months–6 years; 800 kcal and 20–25 g protein/day for severely malnourished children.
Pregnant and lactating women are entitled to 600 kcal and 18–20 g protein/day.
Pediatricians should verify registration, ration receipt, and consumption during OPD visits.
Growth faltering or recurrent infections should trigger referrals to nutrition rehabilitation centers.
reference_quote: Nutritional norms in the NFSA schedule set a minimum of about 500 kcal and 12–15 g protein per child per day for 6 months–6 years, with higher norms (about 800 kcal and 20–25 g protein) for children with severe acute malnutrition.
document_key_topics: Supplementary nutrition entitlements under ICDS and POSHAN 2.0
NFSA nutritional standards for children and women
Pediatric growth monitoring and malnutrition management
Operational challenges in anganwadi nutrition programs
